item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is honestly one of my favorite SCPs. I’ve always found the idea of sunken ships and airplanes, both fascinating and creepy. The idea that a vessel or craft has been hidden far from human reach and left to slowly decay and collapse, alone with it’s secrets, is pretty haunting. That’s what I love most about this SCP. The idea that somewhere, lost in the ocean is a massive airplane, still calling for help. Help which will never come. The people inside are forced to relive their final moments, as if in a looping purgatory. The foundation are too wary to attempt any kind of rescue, out of the fear that the looping time anomaly might affect them as well. Instead the passengers are forced to hopelessly relive their hellish deaths, while praying for an end to come. The plane’s only hope is a single, weathered buoy in the vast ocean.

At least that is how I thought the story went. Upon rereading it today, I have to say that I am a little disappointed. The story isn’t as eerie as I had once thought. Somehow people eating each other like zombies and a sentient plane that kills people with waves, is a bit too over dramatic and cartoony for my taste. It’s the subtle creepiness that I enjoyed. It didn’t need all that extra stuff.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image is from here, which is public domain and thus cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So how far can Subject 56 get in 13 seconds underwater? Even though that's not a lot of time, I imagine that with the wreckage of the plane, eventually, the subject would be able to exit and start traveling away from the site. What happens once that body is outside the plane and open to the waters?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty good, very horrifying and misterious. Really want to know more, and an article that makes you want to see more is usually good.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, is the slot connected to Air Florida flight 90 crashing on 01-13-1982? Seems like too much of a coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what the Hume levels of the anomaly are during its active periods, I have a feeling they would be very wrong, and if they have some kind of advance Hume imaging system similar to infrared they might even spot a strange spike/dip in humes along the wing of the plane

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now the really unnerving question is: if all this warping of reality is just nature trying to fix itself, then what exactly was the thing that caused it like, and what did it do if it was bad enough to make reality itself try and “repair” it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this is personally what an SCP article should be all about. Something that is mildly disturbing, but interesting enough for you to want more. At the end of this all I wanted was another experiment log. Why does it say hunger? Why's the D-Class stuck? Goddamnit tell me…

Yeah this skip is quite old (on series 5 now, woo) but 6 years later I still will give a hearty +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every time I read this, I like it (or is the right word loathe it?) more and more. It starts off weird and disturbing, and it only gets moreso the more I think about it. I really want to know what event led to 1382 manifesting, and what would happen if a robotic drone got caught in the loop. Solid +1 from me.

item.title: What?
item.content:encoded:
Seeing how highly rated made me want to check it out. I read the description, cool, albeit not very reader friendly. Then I see the collapsibles, I think, okay, the big punch and mystery/story/big picture is coming soon, and then the article is over.

I'm left with a not-so-well written description and hunger bits. The zombies are cliche, and the D-Class incident seemed unnecessary. Hard Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing that got my attention was the use of designation numbers. The article never says "SCP-1382 is", even as a collective designation; it only talks about -1, -2, and -3. That made me want to keep reading, to put it together and find out what was going on here. The early reference to the incorporated missing persons and D-class also contributed to that effect.

In the end I concluded that, appropriately for a story surrounding an accident, SCP-1382 is none of these objects or people; it's whatever the hell happened that left them like this. The nature of that event initially seems pretty straightforward- the plane crashed, the people are still sort-of-alive and signaling somehow- but then the evidence pointing to something even weirder started piling up…

I decided to upvote after reading Incident 2, and reaching the realization that 1382-1 is not in fact a request for rescue- or at least, that it's not a request to get the plane and passengers out of the lake. Well done, sir or madam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: bro you're replying to a thing that's like two years old

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might have an idea. You'll need to PM me for it, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just noticed this:

SCP-1382-1 is anchored to SCP-1382-2, which is embedded on the sea floor at a depth of ██ meters.

Isn't this in Lake Michigan?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That ending did give me chills. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An excellent question, and one I hadn't considered when I wrote the entry. If I had to fathom a guess, I'd say that yes, it does absorb nearby fish, but fish skeletons swimming about haven't been noticed yet (I seem to recall fish being cold blooded and therefore wouldn't show up on a heat sig camera) and they repeat themselves as well. But most avoid the area, the way dogs and cats do in ghost stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, now I MUST re-read this whole thing with Shatner's voice. Not sure if I should thank you or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hum. Question. What effects, if any, does this have on the fish near it/do the fish effect it at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll tell you this much. The undeath, the temporal loop, the insanity of the crew and its ability to absorb living beings? Whatever SCP-1382 encountered, it didn't do that to the plane and crew. That phenomenon is nature trying to fix what happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh.. please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's similar, I admit. My hope though is that people aren't sure whether it's a time-distortion phenomenon, or a plane full of ghosts reliving their last moments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it… it reminds me of SCP-176, but not in such a way that I would ever see it as a rip-off. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That could be a good idea. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could post it in the leak thread (http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-561991) in the forum that way only people who want spoilers will go look

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe. Maybe not. I'm on the fence; if I come out and say what happened I might ruin it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was there anything you had in mind for what goes unmentioned in the article that you could put behind a spoiler alert here? Like, what the hell HAPPENED, and what happened to the other four?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'm honestly surprised at how well it's been received.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It's all very strange and WTF, and that works really well for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did consider this. My original idea was an SCP that encompassed the so-called Great Lakes Triangle. The Global Maritime Distress Safety System (implemented in 1999) would be the Foundation's doing, so as to contain 1382. When ships stop responding to S.O.S. signals, the scip wouldn't be able to lure them in. However, I decided to narrow the focus and make 1382 a (supposedly) isolated incident, making the GMDSS reference redundant.

The reason 1382 uses an S.O.S. is because Flight 441 (SCP-1382-2) crashed in the 70's; GPS was only just being introduced back then. Most of them would have understood what an S.O.S. meant and would have used it if they could to signal passing vessels.

And ultimately, "S.O.S." is the 1382's theme. Save our souls. Literally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well-written, and I do like it, but there is one glaring flaw I can't ignore. Morse code hasn't been something ships have bothered to detect for thirteen years.

http://www.cracked.com/article_19867_6-historical-events-happening-more-recently-than-you-think.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had one of my proofreaders mention this to me. I suppose its unavoidable, but I did try to set it apart by making the "fresh" victims aware of their situation but unable to liberate themselves. Hopefully that will stand out for the readers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! For justice!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thankya! Glad I could give you an entertaining chill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you did!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. Reminds me of 176.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How did I miss this.

HOW did I miss this.

Upboated for greatest justice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is creepy as all get-out, specifically the various actions of the reanimated corpses during the reenactment, not to mention those who get sucked into it. I can't remember the last time I read an article this creepy around here!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First time I looked at this, I just wrote it off as being too long winded and uninteresting.

That being said, after a second {and more thorough} readthrough, well done, author. This is really entertaining.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh no, it worked here, I just felt like it needed to be mentioned. You're right, to do what you wanted to do here, you really didn't have much choice. Like I said, though, don't sweat it, you definitely pulled it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose the similarities with 616 are hard to ignore, but it wasn't my intention to do so. 1382 was created from an attempt to explain the Great Lakes Triangle (and perhaps it still is), but I narrowed the scope to make it seem more isolated. If this sort of thing was happening all over the Great Lakes the Foundation wouldn't be able to keep a lid on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. The thing that grabbed me was what actually attacked the liner. It reminded of the Boeing Gate to hell SCP. Well done, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The bodies are skeletons now; it was deduced that 44 and 45 were eating 46 and 47 by their movements: gnawing and tearing at the arms and legs with their jaws.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subjects #44 and #45 overpower Subjects #46 and #47 (both juveniles, estimated to be 8 and 6 years of age, respectively) and begin to consume them.

So are #46 and #47 partially consumed, with #44 and #45 miming eating them? Or does the consumed flesh come back on each activation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made a small correction and revised the list of "SCP-1382-3" activity. I should read a little easier now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hear you 100% man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, let's put it this way; that didn't provoke a "good" (as I would call it) reaction to to the SCP. Instead of really enjoying it I just felt really nauseous…those are kids man. I hate anything to do with kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew if I made them just another loop, it wouldn't be nearly as worth an upvote. It's possible the old crew /aren't/ in a loop: they're just so insane they can't think to do anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks man! That's a GGG moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well look at it this way: whatever attacked them, whatever it did to the plane, was so unnatural that it resulted in an SCP. If you were there when it happened, trying to eat a kid would probably be one of the more logical things going through your mind at the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There were 56 original passengers on the original flight that are unaccounted for (their bodies aren't on the plane). Having absorbed three fishermen and a D-class, the crew compliment is back to 56. Something to think about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my hope. You see all this and it feels so confusing, so it makes you wonder: what was it that attacked them, and what did it do that resulted in THIS?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew a Shatner reference would be inevitable, but what else could I do? lol There's a. Thing! On the. Wing. Of the plane!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I agree, and I hope I can find a way to revise it that feels more natural but still clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do feel that it could use some cleaning, but I'm not sure how yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's weird, it's intriguing, it's creepy as hell. I like it! The greater awareness of some of the subjects is a (for lack of a better word) nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Blast, I was about to write something similar to this. You did it in a much freakier manner than I was going to, though, so i'm okay with this. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The eating children bit…like, da fuck? That doesn't feel necessary, just tacked on to feel horrible. Like, ech.

Otherwise, pretty good, yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What gets me is that it seems to be replacing the "original" passengers/crew with people it's killing/eating. So… what happens when most/all of the originals have been replaced? I like the mystery implied in ALL of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this one - it made the hairs on the back of my neck stand up. The decaying passengers of the original flight reanimating was so-so to me, with all the "eating children" and "cannibalizing noisy passenger's hand" bits. What pushed me to upvote was the concept of the D-personnel being more aware of the situation, and attempting to communicate and escape, only to become inanimate with the rest of them. The sheer horror of being deep underwater, being aware that one is decaying, and attempting to escape whatever is happening down there chills me to the bone.

A part of me wonders what will happen if, due to the Foundation's containment procedures, it cannot "feed" anymore; will it become more aggressive or malicious? Will it widen the range of its effects? Will the actions of the passengers below start to change - become more disturbing, or more self-aware? All the questions just makes it more disturbing to me, and I like that.
